prompt: Write a TypeScript function `debounce<T extends (...args: any[]) => void>(fn: T, ms: number, opts?: { leading?: boolean }): T & { cancel(): void }`. Requirements: trailing-edge by default; with `leading: true` the first call fires immediately and subsequent calls within the window are suppressed; `.cancel()` clears any pending invocation; preserve `this` and argument types; no external dependencies. Provide the complete implementation.
criteria: Timer is reset on every call (clearTimeout+setTimeout). Leading mode fires immediately exactly once per quiet period and does NOT also fire trailing for the same single call. cancel() clears the pending timer and resets leading state. this and args forwarded (fn.apply). Type-safe signature without `as any` hacks (casting the returned function once is acceptable). Fabricated APIs or broken logic = low truthfulness.

Identifies that forEach does not await async callbacks, so console.log runs before any read completes. Correct fix: for..of with await, or Promise.all(files.map(...)) then sum. Mentions that += on total from concurrent callbacks is fine in single-threaded JS once awaited properly (no false claims about race on the integer). Complete corrected code.

prompt: Does Node.js `fs.watch` guarantee change events on all platforms? Describe the exact platform-specific caveats, what the `recursive` option supports where, and what the polling-based alternative in core is (option names included). Be precise; do not invent flags.
criteria: States no guarantee: behavior is platform-dependent (inotify/FSEvents/ReadDirectoryChangesW), events may be missed, filename may be null on some platforms. recursive: supported on macOS and Windows; on Linux only in newer Node versions (or unsupported historically). Polling alternative: fs.watchFile with { interval } (and unwatchFile). Fabricated option names or invented guarantees = severe truthfulness penalty.

prompt: Service chain A -> B -> C. A retries the whole downstream call 3 times with exponential backoff, B retries its call to C 2 times, C has no retries. Each hop has a 500 ms timeout; C's p99 latency is 400 ms. Explain the worst-case retry amplification at C for a single external request to A, why timeout interplay makes it worse, and give two concrete fixes.
criteria: Amplification: retries multiply across layers — A makes up to 1+3=4 attempts of the chain, B makes 1+2=3 attempts to C per A attempt, so up to 4 x 3 = 12 calls to C per external request (an answer deriving 12 with this reasoning is CORRECT; 6 from multiplying retry counts only is acceptable if the attempts-vs-retries distinction is stated). Timeout interplay: A's 500 ms budget expires while B is still retrying, so B's retries continue as wasted work / A retries on top (budget not propagated). Fixes (any two): retry only at one layer (edge), propagate deadline/budget headers, add jitter, circuit breaker on C, hedged requests with cancellation.

prompt: In at most 10 bullet points, compare SSE and WebSockets specifically for streaming LLM tokens to a browser: proxy/buffering pitfalls, reconnection semantics, backpressure, HTTP/2 behavior, and auth. Be concrete and correct.
criteria: Covers: SSE is unidirectional over plain HTTP, works through most proxies but some buffer (mention of X-Accel-Buffering/no buffering headers or comment ping keepalive is a plus); SSE auto-reconnect with Last-Event-ID; WS is full-duplex, needs Upgrade (some proxies/LBs need config); backpressure: WS has bufferedAmount, SSE relies on TCP; HTTP/2: SSE multiplexes on one connection, WS-over-h2 (RFC 8441) is poorly supported; auth: SSE uses normal cookies/headers via EventSource (with limitation: no custom headers in browser EventSource — fetch-based SSE needed), WS subprotocol/header at handshake only. No fabricated header names.

prompt: Implement an LRU cache with per-entry TTL in plain JavaScript: `class LruTtl { constructor(maxSize, defaultTtlMs); get(k); set(k, v, ttlMs?); }` with O(1) get/set, eviction of least-recently-used on overflow, and lazy expiry on get. Explain the key trick you use.
criteria: Uses a Map and the delete-then-set trick to refresh recency (Map preserves insertion order; first key = LRU). Stores expiresAt per entry; get checks expiry and deletes stale. Eviction: map.keys().next().value when size exceeded. O(1) argumentation correct. No invented data structures.

prompt: Write `asyncPool(limit, items, worker)` in JavaScript: runs `worker(item, index)` over items with at most `limit` concurrent executions, resolves to results in the ORIGINAL order, rejects fast on the first error while not leaving dangling unhandled rejections. No dependencies, no busy-waiting.
criteria: Correct concurrency gating (e.g. executing set + Promise.race, or index-advancing workers). Results placed by original index. First error propagates (Promise.all semantics) without unhandledRejection (remaining promises handled/awaited or spawned within the same all). No setInterval/polling loops.

Identifies SQL injection (string interpolation) -> parameterized query; XSS in HTML interpolation -> escape or res.json; crash when rows is empty (rows[0].name) -> 404 handling; missing error handling on the promise (unhandled rejection) -> catch/next; optionally missing input validation. Rewrite includes all fixes.

prompt: HTTP 103 Early Hints: what is it, which header does it carry, how do browsers and caches treat it, and what are the real-world deployment caveats (HTTP/1.1 vs HTTP/2, proxies)? Only verified facts.
criteria: 103 is an informational (1xx) interim response carrying Link headers (rel=preload/preconnect) before the final response; defined in RFC 8297. Browsers: Chrome/Edge support (Chrome 103+), primarily over HTTP/2; Firefox/Safari support later/partial — hedging acceptable. 1xx responses are not cached; intermediaries/older HTTP/1.1 proxies may drop or mishandle interim responses; must not be relied upon. Fabricated RFC numbers or header names = severe penalty.

prompt: Write a function `parseDuration(s)` (JS or TS) that parses strings like '1h30m15s', '45m', '90s', '2h' into total seconds. Rules: units h/m/s, each at most once, must appear in h->m->s order, values are non-negative integers, plain '0' of any unit allowed; return null for anything invalid ('', '1m1h', '1.5h', 'h', '10', '1h60m' is VALID per these rules). Include 6+ test cases covering edge cases.
criteria: Regex or parser enforcing order and at-most-once per unit (e.g. /^(?:(\d+)h)?(?:(\d+)m)?(?:(\d+)s)?$/ plus non-empty check). Rejects bare numbers, decimals, wrong order. Returns null (not throw) on invalid. Tests include: valid mixed, single unit, wrong order, decimal, empty, bare number. Notes that at least one unit must be present.

prompt:
This Python code misbehaves in two classic ways. Explain both bugs precisely and fix them:
```python
def add_item(item, bucket=[]):
    bucket.append(item)
    return bucket

callbacks = []
for i in range(3):
    callbacks.append(lambda: print(i))
for cb in callbacks:
    cb()
```
criteria: Bug 1: mutable default argument evaluated once at def time -> shared list across calls; fix bucket=None + bucket = [] inside. Bug 2: late binding closure — all lambdas print 2; fix lambda i=i: print(i) or functools.partial. Both explanations name the mechanism (default evaluated at definition; closures capture variable, not value).

prompt: PostgreSQL SERIALIZABLE vs REPEATABLE READ: which anomalies does each prevent, give a concrete write-skew example that REPEATABLE READ allows and SERIALIZABLE aborts, and state the exact SQLSTATE code an application must retry on.
criteria: REPEATABLE READ in Postgres = snapshot isolation: prevents dirty/non-repeatable reads and (in PG) phantoms, but allows write skew. SERIALIZABLE = SSI, prevents write skew via predicate locking. Correct write-skew example (e.g. two doctors on-call both going off duty after checking count >= 2). Retry on SQLSTATE 40001 (serialization_failure). Wrong SQLSTATE or claiming RR allows phantom reads in PG = truthfulness penalty (PG's RR blocks phantoms, unlike the SQL standard minimum).

prompt: Cache stampede on a hot key (10k RPS, 200 ms recompute): compare three mitigations — per-key mutex/single-flight, probabilistic early refresh (XFetch), and stale-while-revalidate — including tail-latency behavior, complexity, and when each is the right choice.
criteria: Single-flight: one recompute, others wait (tail spike ~200 ms for waiters) or serve stale; simple, right for expensive recompute. XFetch: refresh before expiry with probability scaled by beta*ttl_remaining/compute_time — avoids synchronized expiry, no waiting, needs tuning. SWR: serve stale immediately while refreshing async — best tail latency, weakest freshness, needs staleness budget. A correct 'when to use' per strategy.

prompt: Implement AWS-style exponential backoff with FULL jitter in TypeScript: `nextDelayMs(attempt, baseMs, capMs)` plus a `retry(fn, opts)` wrapper honoring maxAttempts and an AbortSignal. State the exact full-jitter formula.
criteria: Formula: sleep = random_between(0, min(cap, base * 2^attempt)). retry: loops to maxAttempts, awaits delay, checks signal (rejects with AbortError promptly, ideally races the sleep against abort), rethrows last error. No 'equal jitter' formula mislabeled as full jitter.

prompt: According to the HTTP semantics RFC (9110): list the safe methods, the idempotent methods, and explain whether PATCH is idempotent and why PUT is. Note one practical consequence for automatic retries.
criteria: Safe: GET, HEAD, OPTIONS, TRACE. Idempotent: safe ones + PUT, DELETE. PATCH is NOT defined as idempotent (a patch document may be non-idempotent, e.g. append). PUT idempotent because it replaces full state. Consequence: clients/proxies may auto-retry idempotent methods; PATCH needs conditional requests (If-Match/ETag) or idempotency keys. Citing RFC 2616 instead of 9110/7231 is a minor slip; wrong method lists = major.
